data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-12T01:39:05.135Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were implemented as sync-over-async (blocking on async calls). 

In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing by default for synchronous operations, but you could opt in to sync-over-async behavior.


In .NET 10, that opt-in was still available, letting you call synchronous methods like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` with configuration. But in .NET 11, the sync-over-async support is being completely removed from the Cosmos DB provider, so all those synchronous LINQ operations will throw `InvalidOperationException` with no way to opt back in.

Let me reconsider the exact timeline here. EF Core 9 was when the Cosmos provider first removed synchronous I/O by default and started throwing exceptions, though there was an opt-in mechanism available. EF Core 10 kept that opt-in around. Then EF Core 11 removes it entirely. Since we're in May 2026 now,  .NET 11 is still a few months away, so I'm working with what I know about the trajectory of these breaking changes. The main issue is that all synchronous methods like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` will throw `InvalidOperationException`, so the fix is converting everything to async equivalents. I should be straightforward about what I'm confident in versus what's speculative about the future release.
